coredata.dc:description: Currently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are widely used as antibacterial agents, and their effects are dependent on the reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by photodynamic and peroxidase activities. Nevertheless, the supply of substrates or light greatly limits GQDs application. Besides, due to compensatory mechanisms in bacteria, comprehensive analysis of the molecular mechanism underlying the effects of GQDs based on cellular-level experiments is insufficient. Therefore, N-GQDs with inherent excellent, broad-spectrum antibacterial efficacy under acidic conditions were successfully synthesized. Then, via multi-omics analyses, the antibacterial mechanisms of the N-GQDs were found to not only involve generation ROS but also be associated with changes in osmotic pressure, interference with nucleic acid synthesis and inhibition of energy metabolism. More surprisingly, the N-GQDs could destroy intracellular acid-base homeostasis, causing bacterial cell death. In conclusion, this study provides important insights into the antibacterial mechanism of GQDs, offering a basis for the engineering design of antibacterial nanomaterials.

Inset is photographs of different concentration of N-GQDs (20–50 μg/mL). c FTIR spectra of N-GQDs. d XPS spectra of N1s orbits. e XPS spectra of C1s orbits. f XPS spectra of O1s orbits. g Images of the bacterial colonies formed by E. coli and K. rhizophila with N-GQDs. h The percentage survival of E. coli under the treatment of N-GQDs and sodium benzoate. i The percentage survival of K. rhizophila under the treatment of N-GQDs sodium benzoate. Data points are reported as mean ± standard deviation.

Effect of carboxyl group on antibacterial properties of N-GQDs. a The effect of -COOH form on antibacterial properties of N-GQDs. b The percentage survival of E. coli under the treatment of N-GQDs and sodium benzoate. c XPS spectra of C1s orbits under different pH. d The effect of -CO content on antibacterial properties of N-GQDs. e The percentage survival of E. coli with the content of -CO. f XPS spectra of O1s orbits in different hydrothermal temperature. Data points are reported as mean ± standard deviation.
Antibacterial mechanisms of N-GQDs. Differentially expressed genes and differential metabolites in E. coli.
chematic illustration about antibacterial mechanisms of N-GQDs.

Antibacterial mechanisms of N-GQDs. a O2 •– EPR spectra of N-GQDs under 1 min solar simulator irradiation. b 1O2 EPR spectra of N-GQDs under 1 min solar simulator irradiation. c •OH EPR spectra of N-GQDs under 1 min solar simulator irradiation. d N-GQDs destroyed oxidative stress defense system of E. coli.

Antibacterial mechanisms of N-GQDs. a N-GQDs destroyed intracellular acid-basic homeostasis. b N-GQDs inhibited the synthesis of LPS in E. coli. c-d SEM characterization respectively on E. coli cells without and with N-GQDs treatment. e-f SEM characterization respectively on K. rhizophila cells without and with N-GQDs treatment.

Antibacterial mechanisms of N-GQDs. a N-GQDs intercepted the process of DNA semiconservative replication in E. coli. b-c Agarose gel electrophoresis of 16s rDNA extracted from E. coli (b) and K. rhizophila (c) without (Lane 1) and with (Lane 2) treatment of N-GQDs, M: marker. d N-GQDs inhibited the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle in E. coli. e N-GQDs inhibited glycolysis in E. coli.

head.ce:title: Based on multi-omics technology study the antibacterial mechanisms of pH-dependent N-GQDs beyond ROS

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are widely used as antibacterial agents, and their effects are dependent on the reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by photodynamic and peroxidase activities. Nevertheless, the supply of substrates or light greatly limits GQDs application. Besides, due to compensatory mechanisms in bacteria, comprehensive analysis of the molecular mechanism underlying the effects of GQDs based on cellular-level experiments is insufficient. Therefore, N-GQDs with inherent excellent, broad-spectrum antibacterial efficacy under acidic conditions were successfully synthesized. Then, via multi-omics analyses, the antibacterial mechanisms of the N-GQDs were found to not only involve generation ROS but also be associated with changes in osmotic pressure, interference with nucleic acid synthesis and inhibition of energy metabolism. More surprisingly, the N-GQDs could destroy intracellular acid-base homeostasis, causing bacterial cell death. In conclusion, this study provides important insights into the antibacterial mechanism of GQDs, offering a basis for the engineering design of antibacterial nanomaterials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections pose a severe threat to public health, and the emergence of antibiotic resistance in bacteria has further intensified the therapeutic challenge (Gao and Zhang, 2021; Linklater et al., 2021; Makabenta et al., 2021). Fortunately, nanomaterials including carbon-based nanomaterials, metal-based nanomaterials and metal organic frameworks have shown promising effects in the treatment of bacterial infections (Guan et al., 2021; Marković et al., 2018, 2019; Zhang et al., 2021). Among them, carbon-based quantum dots including carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been extensively studied in recent years as novel alternatives to traditional antibacterial agents (Kováčová et al., 2018; Loh et al., 2018; Mei et al., 2020; Stanković et al., 2018). The antibacterial activities of CQDs mainly involve cell wall damage and disruption of biofilms matrix through ROS generation via photo-excitation to cause bacterial cell death (Kováčová et al., 2018; Marković et al., 2019; Stanković et al., 2018). Moreover, GQDs have long received considerable attention for their remarkable electronic and physicochemical properties (Chung et al., 2021). On the one hand, due to their excellent electron transport property, GQDs exhibit peroxidase-like activity. Sun and his co-workers found that GQDs can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to generate •OH and greatly enhance the antibacterial activity of H2O2 (Sun et al., 2014, 2015). On the other hand, as photosensitizers (PSs), GQDs can achieve results in photodynamic therapy (PDT) through two mechanisms: in the Type I mechanism, the PSs react with a substrate and transfer a proton or an electron to form a radical anion or cation, respectively, such as O2 •– or •OH; in the Type II mechanism, the PSs convert their energy directly to molecular oxygen, which is a triplet in the ground state, to form 1O2 (Chen et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2019; Ristic et al., 2014). Furthermore, GQDs also inhibit bacterial growth via photothermal effects (Mei et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020) or, in the form of GQD sheets, by physically damaging bacteria (Kadian et al., 2020). However, most of the antibacterial activity of GQDs is highly dependent on the use of substrates (e.g., H2O2) or light, which greatly limits the application of GQDs. Therefore, other underlying antibacterial mechanisms need to be further explored to generate ideas for the engineering design of multitarget antibacterial GQDs materials. Additionally, the mechanisms underlying the antibacterial effects of GQDs have been explored on the basis of their influence on microbial morphology, ROS generation, and changes in protein, nucleic acid and ATP levels (Huang et al., 2021; Kuo et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020; Yu et al., 2020). Nevertheless, due to a compensatory mechanism in bacteria, these explorations have not provided a comprehensive understanding of the molecular mechanism underlying the antibacterial effects of GQDs. Recently, quantitative proteomics and real-time quantitative PCR have effectively revealed the antibacterial mechanism of quaternized carbon quantum dots; these materials were found to act on cellular components, disrupt biological processes and interfere with the molecular functions of related proteins (Zhao et al., 2022). Moreover, transcriptomics was used as an important means to study the responses of Pseudomonas aeruginosa to carbon-based nanomaterials. It was found that two-component regulatory systems and central carbohydrate and amino acid metabolism were down-regulated by carbon-based nanomaterials (Mortimer et al., 2018). However, a transcriptomic and metabonomic understanding of the antibacterial effects of GQDs remains lacking.

In this work, as-prepared N-GQDs exhibited high broad-spectrum antibacterial efficacy even without a substrate or light. The antibacterial efficacy was even higher than that of typical acid preservative sodium benzoate. Subsequently, transcriptomic and metabonomic technologies were utilized to reveal the molecular mechanism underlying the antibacterial effect of N-GQDs. Notably, N-GQDs could cause oxidative stress damage through the photodynamic production of ROS, which destroyed the oxidative stress defense system of bacteria. Additionally, the composition of the cell wall, osmotic pressure of the bacterial cell, synthesis of nucleic acids and energy metabolism were adversely affected by N-GQDs, contributing to bacterial cell death. More importantly, N-GQDs could disrupt intracellular acid-base homeostasis by down-regulating the glutamate-dependent acid resistance system. Overall, N-GQDs with inherently broad-spectrum antibacterial activity were successfully synthesized, and their antibacterial mechanisms were further analyzed at the molecular level.

Disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate and sodium phosphate monobasic dihydrate were purchased from Shengao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjing, China). Yeast powder, peptone, agar powder and 2.5 % glutaraldehyde were purchased from Solarbio Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Sodium chloride and ethanol 100 % were purchased from Tianjin Beilian Fine Chemicals Development Co., Ltd. (Tianjing, China). Biowaste regular agarose g-10 was purchased from XHLY company Ltd. (Beijing, China). Trans2KDNAMarker, 6 × DNA Loading Buffer, Nuclease-free Water were purchased from TransGen Biotech Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 2 × TaqPCRMix was purchased from TIANGEN BIOTECH CO., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 5,5-dimethyl pyridine N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidinooxy (TEMPO) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

N-GQDs were synthesized according to previous reports (Zhang et al., 2017). The morphologies of the N-GQDs were characterized by atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dension Icon, Germany). The UV–vis–NIR spectra between 200 nm and 800 nm were measured by a UV–vis spectrometer (Mapada, UV-1200). Zeta potential was were measured by a laser particle size analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS90). Chemical groups of samples were recorded on FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet IS10, Thermo Nicolet Corporation, USA) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo, USA).

The antibacterial effect of N-GQDs was evaluated with gram-positive bacteria Kocuria rhizophila NCTC8340 (Stored in the laboratory) and gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli MC1061 (Huayueyang Biotech Co., Ltd. Beijing, China). The single colony of K. rhizophila and E. coli on solid Luria-Bertani (LB) agar plates were picked and cultured in 5 mL LB liquid medium and cultured at 37 ℃ for 24 h under 150 rpm rotation, respectively. Then 500 μL bacteria suspension was inoculated into a new 5 mL liquid medium and cultured to logarithmic expectation. The plate counting method was used to assessed the antibacterial activities of N-GQDs, which the number of colony-forming units (CFUs) on agar plates were determined. Subsequently, bacterial suspension (OD600 = 0.5) was transferred to 1.5 mL centrifuge tube and N-GQDs (final concentration of N-GQDs was 50–600 μg/mL) or phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (pH = 7.2, 0.01 M/L) were added. After incubating 60 min at 37 °C, the above culture medium was diluted 10,000-fold with PBS. 50 μL of the dilute bacterial suspension was seeded on the LB agar plates, the plates were stayed in a constant temperature incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. The survival rate was calculated by the following formula:

The antibacterial activities of sodium benzoate were evaluated by the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of carboxyl group on antibacterial properties of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Effect of carboxyl group on antibacterial properties of N-GQDs were assessed via two methods. First, the pH value of N-GQDs (pH of 4, 5, 6, and 7) were adjusted through 0.01 M/L disodium hydrogen phosphate or 0.01 M/L sodium dihydrogen phosphate to control the existence state of carboxyl groups. The plate counting method was used to assess the existence state of carboxyl groups on N-GQDs antibacterial effect. The next step is the same as antibacterial activity assessments. Second, 60 mL 5 mg/mL N-GQDs was transferred to 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor in 140 °C and 180 °C oven treatment 6 h. Oxygen-containing functional group was reduced by hydrothermal reduction. Then, the plate counting method was used to assess the effect of carboxyl content on N-GQDs antibacterial effect. The next step is the same as antibacterial activity assessments too.

Bacteria suspension of K. rhizophila and E. coli were cultured to logarithmic expectation, bacteria suspension was diluted with PBS (pH = 7.2, 0.01 M/L) to OD600 = 0.5. Subsequently, 500 μL of bacterial suspension were transferred to 1.5 mL centrifuge tube and N-GQDs (final concentration was 400 μg/mL) or PBS were added. PBS treated bacteria suspension was used as the control. After incubating 60 min at 37 °C, excess N-GQDs were removed via centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 5 min and bacterial cells were washed three times with PBS. Then bacterial cells were fixed at 4 °C with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde for 4 h, the fixed bacterial cells were washed three times with PBS and then dehydrated for 10 min in 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 80 %, 90 %, 96 % and 100 % alcohol series, respectively. Finally, the morphology of bacterial cells was observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM).

Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was used to measure the concentrations of ROS. Electron spin resonance spectra were obtained by electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (A300-10/12, Bruker, Germany) under solar simulator 1 min. Briefly, 2 mL of the N-GQDs (400 μg/mL) was mixed with the spin trapping agent 5,5-dimethyl pyridine N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidinooxy (TEMPO) to a final DMPO and TEMPO concentration of ∼ 0.1 M. DMPO was used to capture •OH and O2 •–, TEMPO was used to capture 1O2. The samples were subsequently irradiated by solar simulator 1 min

DNA of E. coli and K. rhizophila were extracted by TIANamp bacterial DNA Kit (TIANGEN BIOTECH (Beijing) CO., Ltd., China). The obtained genomic DNA were treated with 200 μg/mL N-GQDs for 30 min, then 16s rRNA were amplified by PCR to obtain 16s rRNA fragments (The 16s rRNA gene primers used were 5’-AGAGTTTGATCCTGGCTCAG-3’ (16F27) and 5’-TACGGTTACCTTG TTACGACTT-3’ (16R1492)). The quality of 16s rRNA fragments was determined by 1.5 % agarose gel electrophoresis. The details of PCR procedures were given in the follow:

PCR reaction systems were as follows: 1 μL 16F27, 1 μL 16R1492, 8 μL 2 × TaqPCRMix, 8 μL dd H2O, 2 μL DNA template. PCR reaction conditions adopted were: 40 amplification cycles consisting of 95 °C for 3 min, 95 °C for 30 s, 56 °C for 45 s, 72 °C for 2 min, followed by 72 °C for 10 min.

To evaluate the transcriptional changes of bacteria in response to N-GQDs, E. coli and K. rhizophila were treated with 400 μg/mL N-GQDs for 60 min, respectively, PBS treated bacteria suspension was used as the control. Bacterial precipitation was obtained via centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 5 min and washed three times with PBS. TruSeq™ Stranded Total RNA Library Prep Kit reagent was used to construct cDNA libraries (Meiji Biopharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China). Then cDNA libraries were sequenced on the Illumina Hiseq platform by Meiji Biopharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Statistical analysis of raw counts by DESeq2 (Version 1.24.0) software to obtain differentially expressed genes (p-value < 0.05 & |log2(fold change)| ≥ 1).

To evaluate the metabolite changes of bacteria in response to N-GQDs, E. coli and K. rhizophila were treated with 400 μg/mL N-GQDs for 60 min, respectively, PBS treated bacteria suspension was used as the control. Bacterial precipitation was obtained via centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 5 min and washed three times with PBS. The metabolites were extracted via 400 μL solution mixed methanol and water with a volume ratio of 4:1, which contain 0.02 mg/mL L-2 chlorophenyl alanine. The obtained samples were ground for 6 min with a frozen tissue grinder (− 10 °C，50 Hz), followed by an ultrasound treatment (5 °C, 40 kHz) for 30 min. After, the samples were stored at − 20 °C for 30 min and centrifuged at 13000 × g for 15 min (4 °C). The supernatants obtained were analyzed by Meiji Biopharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) through liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC–MS, Vanquish Horizon syst, Q-Exactive, Thermo Scientific, USA). Statistical analysis of raw data by Progenesis QI(WatersCorporation, Milford, USA) software and metabolites were identified through metabolic database (http://www.hmdb.ca/, https://metlin.scripps.edu/). ropls(R packages) (Version1.6.2) software was used to obtain differential metabolite (p-value < 0.05 & VIP > 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-GQDs and antibacterial activity experiments
ce:section.ce:para: High-quality large-scale N-GQDs with a height of approximately 1.8 nm were synthesized via a previously described method ( Fig. 1a) (Zhang et al., 2017). The as-prepared N-GQDs showed a uniform morphology with excellent monodispersibility, and the N and O atomic rates were approximately 5.98 % and 33.79 %, respectively (Fig. 1b–f, Fig. S1) (Zhang et al., 2017). Due to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, the N-GQDs exhibited excellent stability and dispersion even at a concentration of 5 mg/mL (inset in Fig. 1b, Fig. S2). The O1s peaks were identified as C-O (11.49 %) and -COOH (19.74 %) (Fig. 1f). As substrate-binding sites, oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., -COOH) may improve the antibacterial effect of GQDs (Sun et al., 2015). As expected, the N-GQDs displayed excellent antibacterial activity against the gram-negative bacterium Escherichia coli (E. coli), gram-positive bacterium Kocuria rhizophila (K. rhizophila) and tetracycline-resistant bacterium Bacillus tropicalis in a dose-dependent manner even without exposure to light or substrate (Fig. 1g, Fig. S3a–b, Fig. S4) (Liu et al., 2021). The inhibitory effect intensified with increasing concentration of the N-GQDs. Although negligible antibacterial effects of N-GQDs were observed against E. coli and K. rhizophila when the concentration was 100 μg/mL, the bactericidal rates of 400 μg/mL N-GQDs against E. coli and K. rhizophila were 98.05 % and 98.66 %, respectively. More importantly, the antibacterial effect of the N-GQDs was greater than that attributed to the organic acid preservative sodium benzoate, the inhibition rates of which against E. coli and K. rhizophila were 56.60 % and 41.06 %, respectively when the concentration was 400 μg/mL (Fig. 1h–i). Overall, the constructed N-GQDs exhibited high stability and dispersion with broad-spectrum antibacterial performance in the absence of substrate and light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of oxygen-containing functional groups on the antibacterial properties of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The abundant substrate-binding sites and catalytically active sites (-COOH, and -CO) on the GQD surface play a determining role in the antibacterial activity of GQDs (Sun et al., 2015; Zeng et al., 2018). Hence, the antimicrobial effect can be regulated by changing the dissociation state and content of oxygen-containing functional groups. In our study, the -COOH form (undissociated or dissociated) and the content of -CO were engineering designed by pH adjustments and hydrothermal reduction. The N-GQDs were negatively charged and showed outstanding stability both in the reduced state and at different pH values (Fig. S5). As expected, the N-GQDs exhibited the best antibacterial effect under acidic conditions (pH of 4 or 5) ( Fig. 2a, Fig. S6a–b). When the concentration was 200 μg/mL, the bactericidal rates of the N-GQDs against E. coli and K. rhizophila were 82.19 % and 85.07 %, respectively, and even reached 95.12 % and 100 % when 300 μg/mL N-GQDs were used (Fig. 2b, Figs. S7–S8). More importantly, the antibacterial effect of the N-GQDs was better than that of sodium benzoate. When the pH was 4, the antibacterial effect of the N-GQDs was approximately twofold that of sodium benzoate (Fig. 2b, Fig. S8). However, when the pH was adjusted to be nearly neutral, the N-GQDs lost most of their antibacterial effects. The reason for this could be that in pH range 4–7, the N-GQDs could generate •OH, 1O2 and O2 •– under 1 min of simulated solar irradiation, but with increasing pH, the content of carboxyl groups and the ROS generation capacity decreased (Fig. S9). In addition, in an acidic environment, -COOH on the surface of the N-GQDs could act in the undissociated form, which is more lipophilic and can enter bacterial cells via passive, unfacilitated diffusion (Fig. 2c). In the cytosol, the undissociated acid dissociates to an acid anion (XCOO-) and a proton (Krebs et al., 1983). As a result, protons and XCOO- accumulate intracellularly and eventually contribute to bacterial cell death (Fleet, 1992). The results showed that the form of surface functional group plays an essential role in the antibacterial properties of N-GQDs.

To further confirm the relationship between the antibacterial effect of the N-GQDs and the content of -CO, the content of -CO on the surface of the N-GQDs was regulated by hydrothermal reduction (140 °C and 180 °C). The reduced N-GQDs were well dispersed, with average height approximately 2.0 nm (Fig. S10). With increasing hydrothermal temperature, the -CO content decreased, and the bacteriostatic rate of the N-GQDs also exhibited the same trend (Fig. 2d). When the hydrothermal temperature was 180 °C, the -CO content decreased from 19.74 % to 5.82 %; at this time, the N-GQDs completely lost the ability to inhibit the growth of E. coli and K. rhizophila (Fig. 2e–f, Fig. S11). The results further indicated that -CO plays a key role in the antibacterial activity of the N-GQDs. This may be because -CO and -COOH are the active centers for the generation of ROS, or N-GQDs may be lipophilic under acidic conditions and may more easily enter bacterial cells, or because the N-GQDs could bind to the cell membrane through physical and chemical interactions.

To prevent the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria and gain further insight into the targets of N-GQDs, the antibacterial mechanisms of the N-GQDs were investigated through multi-omics technology. The effects of the N-GQDs on the transcriptomes of the two types of bacteria were studied. The total numbers of clearly differentially regulated genes in the N-GQD treatment groups were as follows: 732 (349 up-regulated and 383 down-regulated) in E. coli and 419 (339 up-regulated and 80 down-regulated) in K. rhizophila ( Fig. 3, Fig. S12). Generally, metabolites are the bridges between genotype and phenotype, and the changes in metabolites can directly reveal the functions of genes. Therefore, a metabonomic analysis was performed to verify the molecular mechanism underlying the antibacterial effects of the N-GQDs on E. coli and K. rhizophila. In E. coli, 116 different metabolites were identified, of which 30 were increased and 86 were decreased, and 170 different metabolites were identified in K. rhizophila, of which 72 were decreased and 98 were increased (Fig. 3, Fig. S13).

Therefore, through differential expression analysis, gene set analysis, differential metabolite analysis, metabolic set analysis and in vitro antibacterial activity validation, the mechanisms underlying the effect of the N-GQDs were proposed as follows: (1) N-GQDs could generate ROS resulting in oxidative stress in bacteria; (2) acidic N-GQDs may enter cells destroying the intracellular pH balance; (3) N-GQDs could bind to the cell membrane/wall affecting the cell membrane/wall; (4) N-GQDs may bind to nucleic acids via hydrogen bonds and π-π conjugation interfering with the synthesis of nucleic acids; and (5) N-GQDs could block the pathway of energy metabolism causing a decline in the production of ATP ( Fig. 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism by which ROS are generated causes oxidative stress
ce:section.ce:para: Due to N-GQDs contain a large number of active sites, when exposed to solar irradiation, N-GQDs could generate ROS, which could damage enzymes (superoxide dismutases (SODs), catalases (CATs)), the glutathione (GSH) system and non-enzymatic antioxidants (vitamin E, ascorbate and so on) in E. coli ( Fig. 5a–c). Notably, 12 differentially expressed genes (yggX, btuE, grxB, sodB, katE, yfcG, yfcF, sodC, ybdK, aroG, aroA, and tkt) were found to be related to oxidative stress in E. coli after N-GQD treatment (Fig. 3a). Importantly, the up-regulation of the yggX gene suggested that the N-GQDs could cause oxidative stress in E. coli (Pomposiello et al., 2003). Besides, the expression of the genes sodB and katE, encoding enzymes regulating ROS degradation, was down-regulated 2.86-fold and 4-fold, respectively, indicating that the N-GQDs could cause the accumulation of ROS in E. coli, leading to cell death (Fig. 5d, Table S1) (Faraj Tabrizi et al., 2018; Jung and Kim, 2003).

In addition, the expression of the yfcG gene and ybdK gene, which are associated with the GSH antioxidant system, was down-regulated 2.99-fold and 2.39-fold, respectively (Table S1) (Kanai et al., 2006), leading to the levels of γ-glutamylcysteine, GSH and oxidized glutathione (GSSG) in E. coli decreasing 1.04-fold, 1.05-fold and 1.14-fold, respectively (Fig. 3b) (Lehmann et al., 2004; Lu, 2013). These results suggested that the GSH system was hindered by the N-GQDs (Fig. 5d).

Moreover, the expression of 2 genes (aroG and aroA) related to the biosynthesis of L-tyrosine, which is important for antioxidant biosynthesis, was down-regulated 4.07-fold and 2.27-fold respectively, in E. coli (Fig. 5d, Table S1) (Davies and E. Davidson, 1982; Kim et al., 2018). As a result, the content of L-tyrosine was reduced 1.12-fold, contributing to the 1.24-fold decrease in the hydroxyphenyllactic acid content, which indicated that non-enzymatic antioxidants were also damaged by N-GQDs (Fig. 3b, Fig. 5d) (Beloborodova et al., 2012).

Similarly, in K. rhizophila, the N-GQDs down-regulated EL275_RS03480 gene expression, which further impaired the enzymatic mechanisms of ROS degradation (Fig. S14a, Table S2). In addition, the content of non-enzymatic antioxidants, especially ergothioneine, was decreased (Fig. S14b) (Burn et al., 2017). Therefore, N-GQDs could cause oxidative stress by generating ROS in the presence of solar light and destroying the oxidative stress defense system of bacterial cells, ultimately resulting in bacterial cell death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism by which intracellular acid-basic homeostasis is destroyed
ce:section.ce:para: According to our results, the N-GQDs exhibited a better antibacterial effect under acidic conditions. N-GQDs may exhibit good lipophilicity, allowing them to enter cells and disrupt intracellular acid-base homeostasis. As expected, the expression of the acid resistance system-related genes gadW, gadX, evgA and evgS (related to the EvgS-EvgA two-component system, which responds to acid stress) in E. coli was down-regulated upon exposure to N-GQDs (Fig. 3a) (Zeng et al., 2021; Nishino et al., 2003). Notably, the expression of the gadW and gadX genes, which are involved in the glutamate-dependent acid resistance system, was down-regulated 4.39-fold and 2.39-fold, respectively. Interestingly, the production of glutamate was also decreased, which could lead to an increase in H+ ( Fig. 6a, Table S1) (Ma et al., 2002; Paudyal et al., 2020; Tucker et al., 2003). Therefore, the N-GQDs impaired the glutamate-dependent acid resistance system of E. coli, destroying intracellular acid-basic homeostasis. Moreover, the levels of basic compounds, such as dihydrozeatin, pantetheine and isonicotinic acid in E. coli were also decreased 1.7-fold, 1.42-fold and 1.12-fold, respectively, which suggested that N-GQDs could consume basic compounds for acid-base neutralization reactions or that N-GQDs could directly destroy basic compounds (Fig. 3b).

As well as in K. rhizophila, the N-GQDs also impaired the glutamate-dependent acid resistance system by decreasing the content of glutamate and consuming the weakly basic metabolite 5-amino-6-(5'-phosphoribitylamino) uracil (Fig. S14b). Overall, intracellular acid-base homeostasis was disrupted by the N-GQDs via a downregulated acid resistance system and a decrease in the levels of basic metabolites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism by which the cell membrane/wall is wrinkled
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that N-GQDs could lead to oxidative stress in bacteria, changes in bacterial morphology were investigated. Excitingly, we found that the N-GQDs could inhibit the synthesis of the cell wall and change the osmotic pressure of the bacterial cell. Notably, the expression of 7 genes (slp, lolB, lapB, rfaC, mrdA, ansB and ptuA) related to the synthesis of cell wall components was down-regulated in E. coli after N-GQD treatment (Fig. 3c). Among them, the down-regulation of lolB gene expression affected the accumulation of the LolA-lipoprotein complex, resulting in growth arrest and cell death (Tanaka et al., 2001). Additionally, related to the biosynthesis of dTDP-L-rhamnose genes, the expression of ansB and ptuA was down-regulated 3.36-fold and 3.16-fold, respectively (Fig. 6b, Table S1) (Allard et al., 2002; Bonthron, 1990; Nadaraia et al., 2001). Hence, the dTDP-L-rhamnose content was decreased (Fig. 3d), which inhibited the biosynthesis of the O-antigen of lipopolysaccharide (LPS), indicating that the N-GQDs could inhibit the synthesis of the cell wall.

In addition, the N-GQDs down-regulated osmotic pressure-related genes and the levels of metabolites that maintained osmotic pressure, resulting in a decrease in osmotic pressure. The yehY, yehX, yehW and ompW genes, which are related to cell membrane osmotic pressure, were expressed at lower levels, interfering with the cell membrane uptake of osmoprotective molecules. (Fig. 3c, Table S1) (Checroun and Gutierrez, 2004; Gil et al., 2007). Additionally, the expression of the betB gene, which is important for betaine biosynthesis, was down-regulated 2.17-fold, leading to a significant decrease in the betaine level (Fig. 3d, Table S1) (Chambers and Kunin, 1987; Holmström et al., 1994). Moreover, as mentioned above, down-regulation of the expression of aroA, ansB and ptuA could result in reduced production of natural osmotic regulators, such as L-isoleucine, L-phenylalanine, L-cysteine, L-histidine, L-aspartic acid, L-tyrosine and L-glutamate (Fig. 3d) (Wiesenthal et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2021). Thus, this may result in insufficient osmolytes to maintain the osmotic pressure of the cytoplasm, which may lead to the destruction of subcellular structures, causing the bacterial cells to wrinkle or even die (Wang et al., 2022). The results were also confirmed by SEM. Without N-GQD treatment, the E. coli cells retained the typical rod-like shape with smooth and distinct cell walls; however, the bacterial surface became rough and wrinkled after treatment with the N-GQDs (Fig. 6c–d).

Compared with E. coli, K. rhizophila exhibited only a slight change after treatment with the N-GQDs (Fig. 6e-f). A possible reason for this is that the N-GQDs could induce EL275_RS00585, EL275_RS09570 and EL275_RS08255 gene overexpression in K. rhizophila, leading to an increase in uridine diphosphate-N-acetylglucosamine (UDP-GlcNAc), UDP-N-acetylmuraminate (UDP-MurNAc) and UDP-N-acetyl-D-mannosamine levels (Fig. S14a–b, Fig. S15, Table S2) (Hancock and Baddiley, 1976; Shaku et al., 2020). Therefore, N-GQDs could inhibit the synthesis of the cell wall and decrease the osmotic pressure of the bacterial cell of E. coli. Nevertheless, K. rhizophila could initiate a defense mechanism to resist the effect of N-GQDs on the cell membrane/wall.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism by which nucleic acid synthesis is disrupted
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs may bind to nucleic acids via hydrogen bonds and π-π conjugation, interfering with DNA semiconservative replication, pyrimidine metabolism and purine metabolism. Notably, 8 differentially expressed genes (dnaC, holC, holA, rnhB, cytR, upp, guaB, yjjG) in E. coli were identified after treatment with N-GQDs (Fig. 3e). Among them, the dnaC, holC, holA and rnhB genes, which are involved in the replication of DNA, were notably down-regulated indicating that the process of DNA semiconservative replication may be hindered by N-GQDs ( Fig. 7a, Table S1) (Cooper et al., 2021; Kobori and Kornberg, 1982). PCR combined with agarose gel electrophoresis showed a bright and clear DNA band (Lane 1) representing the control group, indicating the existence of macromolecular DNA, whereas the DNA from bacteria treated with N-GQDs did not exhibit clear bands (Lane 2), which further indicated that N-GQDs could damage DNA (Fig. 7b–c).

Furthermore, pyrimidine metabolism and purine metabolism in E. coli were equally disturbed by the N-GQDs. The expression of the genes upp and guaB, which are related to the biosynthesis of uridine and guanosine, was down-regulated 3.07-fold and 2.23-fold, respectively (Table S1) (Husnain et al., 2009; Martinussen and Hammer, 1994). Hence, the levels of uridine and guanosine were reduced 1.06-fold and 1.07-fold, respectively, indicating that the synthesis of nucleic acids was disturbed by N-GQD treatment (Fig. 3f). Additionally, the yjjG gene is crucial for hydrolyzing the phosphate group of 5'-nucleotides, and its expression was down-regulated 2.13-fold, suggesting that the N-GQDs disturbed pyrimidine metabolism and purine metabolism in E. coli (Figs. S16–S17, Table S1) (Weiss, 2007). More importantly, the N-GQDs could block certain metabolic reactions, such as those of R00511, R00183 and R00157, leading to the accumulation of cytidine-5'-monophosphate (CMP), deoxycytidine monophosphate (dCMP), cytosine, adenosine 5'-monophosphate (AMP), adenosine 5'-diphosphate (ADP) and hypoxanthine (Fig. 3f, Fig. S18). Therefore, N-GQDs could affect pyrimidine metabolism and purine metabolism to disrupt the biosynthesis of nucleic acids.

Additionally, in K. rhizophila, after N-GQD incubation, the levels of 5'-monophosphate (UMP) and deoxythymidine monophosphate (dTMP) were decreased, disturbing DNA replication and pyrimidine metabolism (Fig. S19b, Fig. S20). These results suggested that N-GQDs inhibited the synthesis of nucleic acids, hampering gene expression and subsequently interfering with biological functions, ultimately leading to bacterial cell death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism by which energy metabolism is inhibited to reduce cellular energy
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, N-GQDs exhibit excellent antibacterial activity under acidic conditions, which could lead to an increase of intracellular pH. The energy obtained from the hydrolysis of ATP is used to transport accumulated protons from the cytosol to maintain pH homeostasis (Holyoak et al., 1999). Therefore, N-GQDs could reduce energy metabolism, including via the glycolysis pathway, the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle and oxidative phosphorylation, resulting in bacterial cell death. There were 7 differentially expressed genes (coaA, mdh, pgk, pgi, eno, fbaA and fbaB) detected in E. coli after N-GQD treatment (Fig. 3e). Among them, the expression of the pgi, fbaA, fbaB, pgk and eno genes, which are crucial for the glycolysis pathway, was down-regulated after treatment with the N-GQDs, indicating that N-GQDs may hinder the glycolysis pathway and reduce the generation of ATP (Fig. 7e, Table S1) (Zhang et al., 2020). Moreover, the expression of both coaA gene and mdh gene, which participate in the TCA cycle, was up-regulated 2.1-fold, leading to a 1.07-fold increase in the CoA level and a 1.08-fold increase in the malic acid level after N-GQD treatment of E. coli (Fig. 3f, Table S1) (Park et al., 1995; Sibon and Strauss, 2016; Song and Jackowski, 1992). Hence, N-GQDs could cause the accumulation of NADH in E. coli, disrupting the balance of the TCA cycle (Fig. 7d) (Usui et al., 2012). Similarly, N-GQDs could impede oxidative phosphorylation and the TCA cycle in K. rhizophila via down-regulating the expression of related genes (EL275_RS05965, EL275_RS03480, EL275_RS05960, EL275_RS05955, EL275_RS05980, EL275_RS08645, EL275_RS08640, EL275_RS03405, EL275_RS03400, and EL275_RS07345) and reducing the levels of related metabolites (malic acid, citric acid and isocitrate) (Fig. S19a–b, Fig. S21–S22, Table S2). Therefore, the N-GQDs disturbed the energy metabolism of E. coli and K. rhizophila to induce a decrease in the production of ATP, causing the death of the bacteria.

In conclusion, we developed highly stable and dispersed N-GQDs as antibacterial agents and revealed their antibacterial mechanisms based on multi-omics technology. In an acidic environment, the N-GQDs exhibited excellent antibacterial activity due to the presence of surface carboxyl groups. The N-GQDs influenced the expression of 732 genes and 116 metabolites in E. coli, and the expression of 419 genes coupled with 170 different metabolites in K. rhizophila. Hence, the N-GQDs achieved antibacterial effects mainly through oxidative stress, changing the cell membrane and cell wall, destroying the intracellular acid-base homeostasis, interfering with the synthesis of nucleic acids and affecting energy metabolism causing a decline in the production of ATP. This study provides a theoretical basis for the further development of GQDs as antimicrobial agents.

Generally, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as novel alternatives towards traditional antibacterial agents have been widely used for combating bacteria. However, the supply of substrates (e.g., H2O2) or light greatly limits the application of GQDs in nanomedicine, food preservation, etc. Therefore, we developed N-GQDs with inherent excellent, broad-spectrum antibacterial efficacy that are beneficial for removing pathogenic bacteria in the environment. In addition, we studied the molecular mechanisms underlying the antibacterial effect of the N-GQDs via multi-omics analyses, contributing to further comprehension of their harmful effects on bacteria, and offering a basis for the engineering design of antibacterial nanomaterials.
